data.title: Arm Stock Could Win As Agentic AI Shifts The Bottleneck To CPUs
data.description:
Earlier this month, Arm Holdings plc (ARM) unveiled an AGI CPU to address one of AI’s biggest bottlenecks, which is orchestration. During the chatbot craze of 2023-2025, GPUs did most of the heavy lifting while CPUs had become an afterthought. Yet with agentic workloads, which is perhaps the single largest catalyst on the horizon for the AI trade in 2026 and beyond, the importance of CPUs is set to increase.

In agentic workflows, the GPU still handles inference, but between each inference call, the CPU is doing the orchestration - which are best described as handling tool calls, API requests and memory tasks. AI agents are surfacing this new constraint, which is how to prevent latency and underutilized GPUs following the exponential growth of orchestration needs.

For investors, what matters is that CPUs account for 50% to 90% of total latency in workflows, which means the CPU-to-GPU ratio in AI clusters will need to increase. Earlier this year, both Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) and Intel (INTC) saw analyst upgrades based on the outstripped supply of CPUs leading to higher average sales prices of roughly 10% to 15%. Reuters also reported that Intel’s unfulfilled orders are reaching longer than six months while AMD delivery times are believed to be eight to 10 weeks.

Regarding how Arm fits in, the company’s expertise in lowering power requirements could matter more than the market expects. After years of supplying the architecture IP behind other companies’ CPUs, Arm is preparing to directly compete with its customers and x86 CPU competitors by transitioning to a chip designer themselves. This comes during a time when CPU cores are expected to go up 4X from 30 million CPU cores per gigawatt to 120 million CPU cores per GW.

Brief History of Arm

We covered Arm two years ago in our free newsletter, Arm Stock: AI Chip Favorite Is Overpriced, and how its mobile background and power-efficient focus positioned it well for increased AI adoption.

Arm offers the most popular CPU architecture in the world with more than 350 billion chips shipped since its inception, with the company essentially building a monopoly in the mobile CPU market with 99% market share stemming from its "heterogenous compute" design and RISC architecture.

This design helped facilitate lower power requirements as the architecture allows different CPU parts to work together for improved efficiency, with workloads to be allocated across both high-performance and low-performance CPU cores to lower energy by balancing performance.

Arm is translating this expertise in power efficient chips to the data center, powering both Nvidia’s (NVDA) Grace and Vera CPUs, as well as custom CPUs from Amazon (AMZN), Google (GOOGL) and Microsoft (MSFT). For example, Google says its Axion CPUs can offer up to 65% better price-performance and 60% better energy efficiency versus x86 alternatives, while Microsoft’s Cobalt 100 and Amazon’s Graviton4 CPU both have shown significant performance and price-performance advantages over competing x86 products.

The Role of CPUs in Agentic AI and the Coming 4X Increase in CPU Cores

Agentic AI represents a natural evolution from the query-and-response based nature of chatbots, to a more complex system capable of running dozens of different tasks and tools autonomously to reason through a problem and provide a response.

As a result, CPUs will play a more integral role in agentic and multi-agent systems to help solve how the system will schedule dozens of concurrent API requests and tool calls across independent agents, as quickly as possible with minimal delay. This is the orchestration constraint: how dozens of agents can make hundreds of concurrent requests needed to complete their independent workflows without causing significant latency or GPU underutilization.

Multi-agent systems are also expected to drive an exponential increase in token generation, which Arm estimated at up to a 15X increase in tokens per user, due to the increase in tool calls and API requests associated with each agent. This is expected to drive CPU core demand much higher, at a time where key x86 suppliers AMD and Intel battle growing supply constraints.

Arm CEO Rene Haas detailed at the company’s latest "Arm Everywhere" event that a typical AI data center of today will feature around 30 million CPU cores per GW. Solving the flow bottlenecks of agentic inference will require “CPUs near the head node, CPUs next to the accelerator rack, more CPU racks inside the data center,” driving CPU core demand as much as 4X higher to 120 million cores per GW, per Haas.

CPU-Centric AI Systems

We are seeing evidence of how agentic AI and the importance of CPUs are beginning to affect incoming architecture designs. If you did not catch last week’s free stock newsletter, Nvidia Stock Prediction: The Path to a $20 Trillion Market Cap is Strengthening, I want to relay the importance of one sentence:

The goal is no longer to simply sell faster and more powerful chips, but to deliver superior economic value at the system level relative to custom silicon (in other words, let the battle begin).”

Think of the Groq LPX racks that cost Nvidia a record-breaking $20 billion that it is soon deploying, addressing the memory-intensive decode phase of inference to significantly accelerate token throughput. This system-level focus is now moving to CPUs, with Nvidia pivoting to deploy its Grace and Vera CPUs as standalone racks. Nvidia says that when:

paired with Rubin GPUs as a host CPU, or deployed as a standalone platform for agentic processing, Vera enables higher sustained utilization by removing CPU-side bottlenecks that emerge in training and inferencing environments."

Arm’s big announcement was not just that it was launching its first in-house silicon and CPU rack platform, but that it will now be directly competing on the standalone CPU system side. Arm’s foray into the standalone rack market offers a new choice for hyperscalers and data center operators to seamlessly deploy CPU-centric racks alongside GPUs, customize the CPU-to-GPU ratio to optimize for agentic orchestration and enable power-efficient AI inference at scale from start to finish. It will also provide another outlet to avoid vendor-lock in to Nvidia’s ecosystem with both air-cooled and liquid-cooled CPU racks.

Key Advantages of Arm’s "AGI CPU" for Agentic AI Workloads

Arm also marked its long-awaited foray into physical chip development with its "AGI CPU," launched at its Arm Everywhere event last week. The company’s pivot into physical CPU and rack development is one the AI industry will watch with great anticipation given Arm’s history of owning significant IP in the mobile space combined with the company setting out to solve agentic AI’s orchestration challenges.

Leveraging Arm’s history of delivering high performance with low power requirements for mobile devices, the new AGI CPU is designed to offer a similar balance between high performance and low power consumption.

The AGI CPU was co-developed with key partner Meta, the chip’s first customer, who revealed they turned to Arm almost two-and-a-half years ago to see if there was a CPU option that fit Meta’s needs: “put in a lot more cores per watt, but we do not want to compromise on the performance piece.” Meta had only been finding options satisfying one of the two criteria: meeting the performance but with too much power, or meeting the power but with too little performance.

2X Performance of x86 and Record CPU Rack Density

The new CPU features up to 136 of Arm’s highest-performing Neoverse V3 cores, drawing just 300W of power in a single-unit (1U) dual-node server (blade) featuring two chips. This compares to x86 chips, such as AMD’s fifth-gen EPYC CPUs, which deliver 128 to 192 cores per chip but at 390W to 500W in a two-unit (2U) rack.

In an air-cooled rack, Arm can pack 30 blades (or 60 CPUs) for a total of 8,160 cores in a 36kW power envelope, saying this configuration can deliver up to 2X the performance per rack versus x86 chips based on its internal estimates. Arm says this 30-blade design is “setting records for air cooled” racks that is not feasible with other systems, as power consumption is too high.

Arm is taking this a step further with a fully-liquid cooled, 200kW open-standard rack in partnership with Super Micro, packing 168 blades, or 336 CPUs, delivering a total of up to 45,696 cores. Arm EVP of Cloud AI Mohamed Awad stated that while it is a “200-kilowatt rack. We actually will consume about half that much power. We ran out of space. That’s why we couldn’t put more cores in there.”

This is one of the key advantages – it is not just about offering 2X the performance of x86 chips, but providing that performance boost while freeing up power for more compute or for more networking:

So if you have a CPU that can draw less power, it could be just as performant, but use less power, it means you have more leftover for everything else that you want to do. That means more inference and more compute. That means more intelligence.”

One Petabyte of Memory and Low-Latency Chip Design

However, Arm architected the new chip with other key optimizations in mind, notably on the memory side. The AGI CPU features 96 PCI Gen6 lanes with CXL 3.0 connectivity, which Arm says allows the new CPU to be attached to any accelerator of choice, allowing flexibility of deployments with Nvidia or AMD GPUs or custom chips.

The chip also features up to 6TB of memory, providing more than 1 petabyte (PB) of low-latency memory in the liquid-cooled 200kW rack. Arm is achieving this extreme low latency of <100 nanoseconds from memory via a dual chiplet design, with each chiplet having both the memory and I/O directly onboard to avoid multiple links across the silicon.

Arm says the new chip’s performance advantage over x86 could enable “up to $10B in capex savings per GW of AI data center capacity,” making it a compelling option for current and future agentic AI-optimized deployments to save money, save power and avoid Nvidia-lock in from its accelerator-agnostic nature.

Looking beyond AMD and Intel, Arm’s in-house design may also outperform Nvidia’s design based on Arm’s IP as the AGI CPU offers a step up in core count and memory at the rack level versus Nvidia’s Vera CPU, despite Vera running on custom Arm "Olympus" cores.

Nvidia says the Vera CPU rack features 256 CPUs, each packing 88 cores for a total of 22,528 cores across the rack; this offers 45,056 threads with multi-threading. Vera racks will also offer up to 400 TB of memory capacity with 315 TB/s of memory bandwidth with PCIe Gen 6 lanes and CXL 3.1 connectivity, whereas Arm offers 2.5X the capacity though just ~274 TB/s of bandwidth at 6GB/s per core.

Quantifying the Impact of CPU-Optimized Agentic AI

Arm’s executives provided some initial commentary about long-term growth projections, seeing the new CPU as a rather lucrative revenue opportunity by the turn of the decade.

Arm expects the AGI CPU to generate $1 billion in revenue in fiscal 2027 and 2028 (June 2026 to March 2028), with the first generation of the AGI CPU now available and ramping further in 2H.

Arm outlined a second generation of the chip tentatively on deck for 2027, likely contributing to some of the revenue generation in 2028 and beyond. On a broader long-term view, Arm estimates the AGI CPU line could generate $15 billion in revenue in 2031, or ~60% of its 2031 revenue target of $25 billion, with a third generation potentially launching before then, though Arm provided no timeline for that chip.

This represents around a 15% share of what Arm believes will be a $100 billion TAM for data center CPU. This is a higher TAM than what Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang discussed at GTC, saying:

“I'm not expecting CPUs to be that much and call it because CPUs just don't add up to much, okay. And so you could say CPU is another 5%”

– or around a $50 billion TAM on $1 trillion.

The challenge in scaling from a fresh launch to $15 billion in revenue in a matter years is definitely doable, considering the current pace of capex growth and tens of billions in AI accelerator revenue flowing to Nvidia, AMD and Broadcom (AVGO) each quarter. However, the path ahead is not free of challenges, as Arm must still compete against Nvidia’s new standalone deployments, as well as the custom Arm-based CPUs hyperscalers have already built – Amazon’s Graviton, Google’s Axion, and Microsoft’s Cobalt.

Financials

Arm’s Financials Offer Superior Gross Margins

The company’s licensing and other revenue grew by 25% YoY and down (2%) QoQ to $505 million in FQ3. The revenue growth decelerated from 56% YoY and 10% QoQ growth in the previous quarter. Licensing revenue has been lumpy, and management mentioned on the earnings call that it varies quarter to quarter due to the timing and size of high-value deals. So, annualized contract value or ACV is considered a key indicator of the underlying licensing trend. ACV grew by 28% YoY and 1% QoQ to $1.62 billion in FQ3. ACV grew by 28% in the past three quarters.

AGI CPU Could Become a $15 Billion Revenue Line by FY2031

Arm is launching its own chip which will help the company grow revenues and profits. The company expects more than $1 billion in revenue from this business over the next two years with the majority of revenue to be recognized in FY2028 ending March.

Arm expects an exponential ramp to $15 billion in revenue in FY2031. The strong growth is primarily driven by solid demand and the increasing complexity of chips will lead to a significant rise in average selling prices. Gross margins are expected to be at least 50% and the adjusted operating margin of over 30% for the chip business.

The company’s licensing and royalty revenue is expected to be $10 billion in FY2031 ending March, with a gross profit margin of 99% and over 65% adjusted operating margin. Management increased this long-term adjusted operating margin guidance by 500 basis points.

The company’s total revenue in FY2031 is expected to be $25 billion and adjusted EPS is expected to be over 9. The current consensus revenue estimates for FY2031 of $21.18 billion and adjusted EPS of $8.39 are lower by 18% and 7.3%, respectively.

Royalty Revenue Is Entering a Sustained 20% CAGR Phase

The company’s royalty revenue has grown at about a 14% CAGR in the past five years. It has accelerated to over 20% in the past two years as Armv9 and Compute Subsystems (CSS) have started to ramp. Looking forward, management expects that the royalty revenue Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR) will be 20% over the next five years. The company’s royalty rates have doubled from Armv8 to Armv9 architecture, and again to CSS.

Valuation

Arm is currently trading at a P/S ratio of 36.3 and a forward P/S ratio of 28.6. The company is trading significantly higher than its other semiconductor peers like Broadcom’s forward P/S ratio of 14.4 and Nvidia’s forward P/S ratio of 11.6.

The company’s revenue growth is expected to accelerate in the next five years compared to the previous period. The company’s revenue CAGR has been 19.3% from FY2021 to FY2026E. Analysts expect revenue to grow at a CAGR of 34% from FY2026E to FY2031E and will be even higher at 38.5% if we use the $25 billion management guidance. However, when looking at the AI segment of many semiconductor peers, the growth rate does not stand out, per se, to justify the high valuation. Rather, the consistency of licensing and royalties' revenue does stand out, and this recurring revenue will create a nice baseline when you combine higher growth from their merchant CPUs.

Cash Flows

The company’s cash flows have been lumpy due to high working capital and high capex to support the long-term growth. However, with the expected strong future profit growth, the cash flows should improve. The company also has a strong balance sheet with cash & short-term investments of $3.54 billion and no debt.

Conclusion:

Arm may be a right place-right time stock, to where the years where Arm sat uneventful compared to peers could payoff as they are more prepared to aggressively compete in the AI market. The valuation is an unknown, and an important aspect given merchant CPU revenue will take time to scale.

To offset this, Arm offers consistent (and rare) recurring revenue, strong margins and far less speculation than, say, an AI software stock that must invest heavily to remain competitive and prove they can build user traction. Instead, Arm offers decades of IP excellence, a 99% penetration in mobile, all while its x86 competitors like AMD and Intel struggle to keep up with their growing backlogs.

The AI trade has been in question lately, yet when the market regains confidence in AI again, Arm will undoubtedly be part of a select, core AI semiconductor pack positioned to deliver for the long haul.

Please note: The Tech Insider Network conducts research and draws conclusions for the company’s portfolio. We then share that information with our readers and offer real-time trade notifications. This is not a guarantee of a stock’s performance and it is not financial advice. Please consult your personal financial advisor before buying any stock in the companies mentioned in this analysis.

data.title: JIVE: Leading International Value ETF Heavily Weighted In Financials
data.description:
Fast Facts About The JPMorgan International Value ETF

The JPMorgan International Value ETF (JIVE) is an actively managed ETF launched on September 13, 2023, with an objective of long-term capital appreciation. JIVE has a portfolio of 337 companies, a trailing 12-month yield of 2.67%, a 30-day SEC yield of 3.12%, and an expense ratio of 0.55%. Distributions are paid annually. It is quite a large and liquid ETF, with about $2 billion in assets under management (“AUM”) and an average daily dollar trading volume of $30 million. The fund’s sponsor, JP Morgan (JPM), is a leading global financial services firm with about $3.9 trillion of assets.

Strategy

As described in the prospectus by JP Morgan, the fund may invest in both developed (ex-U.S.) and emerging markets and intends to maintain geographic and sector exposures similar to those of the MSCI ACWI ex USA Value Index. Value companies are primarily identified based on price/sales, price/earnings, and price/net assets ratios. The investing process blends quantitative screens and in-depth fundamental analysis. The fund may also invest in ETFs and use derivatives for investing and hedging purposes. Exposure in certain currencies may be hedged with currency forwards from time to time, even though the fund is generally not currency-hedged.

The portfolio’s turnover rate was 46% in the most recent fiscal year. This article will use the iShares Core MSCI Total International Stock ETF (IXUS) as a benchmark. IXUS tracks the MSCI ACWI ex USA IMI Index.

The JIVE Portfolio

The portfolio is mostly invested in large- and mega-cap companies (about 75% of asset value), with notable exposure in Japan (15.3%) and the U.K. (10.7%). The geographical allocation is close to that of IXUS, although JIVE significantly downplays Canada and Taiwan.

The fund has a focus on financials (34.5% of asset value), in particular banks (22.5%). The second heaviest sector (energy) weighs only 12%. Compared to the benchmark, JIVE overweights financials and energy, while it mostly downplays technology and industrials.

The portfolio is well-diversified, with low company-specific risk. The top 10 issuers, listed in the next table, represent 15.4% of asset value, and the largest position weighs 2.65%.

data.title: Meta: Hated Again, And That's An Opportunity
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) shares are down 28% from their peak last August and are now officially in correction territory. There are three issues weighing down on the ticker:

  1. Geopolitical tensions, oil prices, and related broad-market factors.
  2. Legal setbacks.
  3. Surging CapEx and AI spending (including the failure of Llama - Meta's Large Language Model "LLM" - to catch up with peers).

The tail risk is material. The recent court rulings in the United States threaten the core ad business by questioning the design and the impact of "addictive features" on society. In Europe, regulators are asking the social media giant to give users greater control over their data.

This is not the first time that the market has put Meta's business model under scrutiny. If history is any guide, periods of peak skepticism are followed by a rebound. For long-term investors willing to take the tail risk, Meta's current share price dip is an opening.

Legal Setbacks And Ad Segment Performance

In 2021, Apple (AAPL) shook the digital ads market when it implemented the App Tracking Transparency "ATT" framework, which required users to actively opt in to allow companies to track their activities across apps and websites. Meta used this data to provide key ad services such as measuring ad conversion, proving returns, and offering targeted ads. Naturally, the ATT framework caused concern, pulling shares down to the ~$90 level by late 2022. The rest is history. Meta persevered by leaning on statistical and AI models, in addition to sourcing data from its ad customers to fill in the gap created by the ATT framework. By 2025, advertising revenue reached a record of $196 billion, up 22% YoY. Ad impressions rose 12%, faster than the growth in daily active users "DAU," which grew at 7%. Finally, price per ad rose by 9% in 2025, on top of a 10% rise in 2024.

The graph below shows an index of Meta's Price per Ad built using the company's reported % change in Price/Ad.

The recent legal setbacks are bringing Meta's business model back under scrutiny. The company might need to change its app design and ad platform again to accommodate privacy concerns and harmful "addictive features," such as infinite scroll and autoplay.

In April 2025, the European Commission fined Meta €200 million, citing that the company's earlier privacy remedy forced EU customers into an ultimatum: 1) Agree to share personal information and access Meta's apps for free, or 2) Pay a fee to opt out of data sharing. A new model was launched in January 2026, giving EU users an option to receive non-personalized ads and still access Meta's services for free. During the Q4 '25 Earnings Call, the company's CFO told investors that recent changes could create some headwinds.

Secondly, Meta is grappling with a myriad of cases tied to youth addiction, child safety, and mental health. Plaintiffs argue that apps such as Instagram are designed to keep kids and teens hooked. They also argue that the apps have caused mental distress and illness. The company lost a bellwether case last month when a jury sided with the plaintiff, holding Meta and Alphabet responsible for a young woman's addictive behavior. These cases suggest that Meta might change some of its app features that support its growth and user engagement.

AI Is Not Just LLMs

Meta increased its 2026 CapEx budget significantly from $72 billion to $125 billion (at midpoint). This raised renewed concerns over ROI and how it plans to monetize these investments.

What the market is discounting here is that a significant part of that CapEx spending is for Meta's core ad business. The company is increasingly incorporating AI for ad placement and targeting. AI is now being deployed to fill in the gaps created by ever-tighter privacy policies.

Last year, management implemented the Generative Ads Model "GEM," a platform "built on an LLM-inspired paradigm" according to Meta (but not an LLM).

The first area is our ad system, where we're continuing to scale the complexity and size of our models to better select which ads to show. In Q4, we doubled the number of GPUs we used to train our GEM model for ads ranking. [...] The GEM and sequence learning improvements together drove a 3.5% lift in ad clicks on Facebook and a more than 1% gain in conversions on Instagram in Q4 - Susan Li, CFO, Q4 '25 Earnings Call.

Some of that CapEx is also channeled towards initiatives that increase engagement, including content recommendations and even computing capacity to support higher engagement on new services such as Reels, which saw watch times increase by 30% in 2025.

That's why, despite increased CapEx and R&D, the ROA hasn't materially declined.

It is important to note that Meta hasn't fully incorporated LLMs into its ad platform yet. CEO Mark Zuckerberg said that a new LLM-powered ad platform is in the works. When complete, it will make the current one look primitive.

We're also working on merging LLMs with the recommendation systems that power Facebook, Instagram, Threads, and our ad system. Our world-class recommendation systems are already driving meaningful growth across our apps and ads business, but we think that the current systems are primitive compared to what will be possible soon. Mark Zuckerberg, CEO, Q4'25 Earnings Call.

The key point here is that the recent productivity gains are a result of AI enhancements, but not necessarily LLM. That's a separate initiative that is yet to become profitable, but nonetheless, it is strategically important. For a company of Meta's caliber, with a consumer-facing role, having control over such a key feature of the Internet is prudent. Meta's recent investments in developing Llama suggest that it intends to stay in the race, despite recent hiccups that saw its Llama model lagging behind those of competitors.

Valuation

Losses of Reality Labs are significant, representing roughly 32% of net income as of 2025. Equally important, the segment is not critical to Meta's core ad business. This makes it easy to imagine Meta without Reality Labs, particularly the part tied to the Metaverse. In fact, Meta is already trimming part of that business.

For the sake of this argument, let's imagine that Meta axes the segment. In FY'25, Reality Labs carried a net loss of $19 billion. Removing that would have increased consolidated net income from $60.4 billion to $79 billion, up roughly 32%. On an EPS basis, that would have brought diluted EPS to $30.8/share, up from $23.5/share. Based on the current share price of 573, that's a P/E ratio of 18.6x, bringing the company's valuation below the Communications sector median of 18.9x.

Historically, Meta has succeeded in delivering solid growth, primarily driven by its core business. Digitalization trends and ad efficiency improvements helped support net income growth. On a forward basis, PEG stands at 0.84x, one of the bright spots in Seeking Alpha's Quant system, which grades Meta an F on valuation.

Finally, although Meta trades at a forward P/E multiple of 18.9x (about 40% above the sector's median), this ratio falls below the company's 5-year historical average when Meta traded at 22.6x forward P/E.

Ray-Ban Meta

The Ray-Ban Meta glasses have the potential to become a hit. Some are skeptical given the long history of failed attempts in this category - including Google Glass, Snap Spectacles, and Amazon Echo Frames. This time seems different.

Meta's glasses are designed in partnership with Ray-Ban, part of EssilorLuxottica (ESLOF), a leading glass frame provider known for its sleek and modern designs. This is a key differentiator when compared to the competition. Sometimes a product's success is more about design and convenience than the embedded tech.

Take a look at Snap Spectacles below and compare them with Ray-Ban Meta above. One is sleek, slim, and subtle, while the other is bulky and too wide on the sides.

Google Glass was light and slim, but it made its wearer look awkward.

Finally, the growth rates of Ray-Ban Meta are spectacular, a sign of market acceptance. The price also seems affordable (~£300 here in the UK, or roughly $400). Customers can try the glasses virtually at the Meta shop.

Risks

Meta faces real challenges as it grapples with multiple fronts. The most serious issue is the privacy and child safety cases, where Meta has suffered legal setbacks that could result in material damage and restitution payments. Perhaps more importantly, the expected changes in Meta's app designs to accommodate the plaintiffs' grievances, such as addictive features and privacy, could hit ad performance.

A further share price decline in response to these dynamics is possible.

For long-term investors, the company's strategic initiatives, including investing in LLMs and the plan to incorporate this technology in the ad platform, could fill the gaps created by recent legal setbacks.

Final Thoughts

Investors should expect a tough year ahead. The company is raising its CapEx and R&D budgets, which should weigh down on profits and FCF in the short run. Meta expects 2026 expenses to be between $162 and $169 billion, a significant jump from the $117 billion reported in 2025. Wall Street was quick to adjust EPS estimates, which now fall at $30.3, up marginally from $29.7 in 2025.

For investors, ultimately, I think Meta will rebound. However, preparing for a further decline in the share price by adopting dollar-value averaging could prove wise in this market.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: FTQI: 11.7% Yielding Covered Call Fund Fixes Some Category Problems
data.description:
First Trust Nasdaq BuyWrite Income ETF (FTQI), the exchange-traded fund that we will be talking about today, has been operational since 2014. It has around $791 million in net assets and invests primarily in the securities of the Nasdaq Composite Index. The ETF's primary aim is to provide current income to its unitholders. Its distributions are made on a monthly basis and currently yield around 12.26% (market price $20.26, last distribution $0.2070). Based on historical trends, FTQI changes the payout amount every calendar quarter.

Changes notwithstanding, the yield has comfortably remained in the double digits over the last year.

If your immediate thought is that the fund cannot possibly distribute this generously by holding equities from the Nasdaq, you get a cookie. You also can rest assured that your thinking is spot on. The primary source of these monthly distributions is the premiums it gets from selling call options on the Nasdaq-100 Index (NDX).

The premiums received from the sale of call options are expected to the Fund's primary source of income. The Fund does not target a specific income level, but seeks to provide investors with current income primarily from options premiums through writing calls with a notional value of 50-100% of the Fund's assets.

Source: Summary Prospectus

This is an actively managed fund, with liquidity, market capitalization, market price, and sector playing a role in the equity selection criteria. The overall risk and reward are also factored in before a security becomes an FTQI holding. The top 10 holdings as of April 2 were a little over 40% of the portfolio.

Apple Inc. (AAPL) and NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) lead the pack, followed by Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD), and Costco Wholesale Corporation (COST), rounding out the top 5. Unsurprisingly, close to half the portfolio is invested in Information Technology.

Most of the remaining funds are invested in Communication Services, Consumer Discretionary, Consumer Staples, Health Care and Financials. With regard to the option holdings, FTQI's strategy is to sell slightly out-of-the-money (OTM) call options on the aforementioned index. The option expirations fall between one and three months. The most recent data published on the fund's website is as of February 27 and gives a glimpse of the strategy in action.

If we look at the detailed listing of the current holdings (as of April 2), FTQI's current option holdings look like this.

While the option overwrite percentage remains close to what it was on Feb 27, the calls are further out of the money (average moneyness) due to the recent downtrend. If this holds until April 17, most of the calls will expire worthless, enabling the fund to pocket the premiums and write a fresh set of options. FTQI's option strategy of selling OTM calls expiring between 1 and 3 months differs from that of its benchmark, the Cboe Nasdaq-100 BuyWrite Index, and is stated in its literature.

This approach contrasts with the benchmark’s option strategy, which employs a 100% overwrite using at-the-money one-month calls that are rolled on the third Friday of each month.

Source: 2025 Annual Shareholder Report

In fact, that helped it outperform the index in 2025.

Performance

As we were saying, FTQI comfortably outperformed with its 12.53% returns versus the 7.87% for the index.

This feat is even more impressive when we consider that the ETF has expenses (0.76% annually), whereas the index has none. The reasoning for this outperformance is explained in the 2025 Annual Shareholder Report referenced above and is reproduced below (emphasis ours).

This outperformance was the result of the Fund’s equity option strategy outperforming the benchmark’s equity option strategy. While the Fund’s underlying equity strategy underperformed the benchmark’s equity strategy, this was more than offset by the equity option outperformance...On average, the Fund’s equity portfolio was underweight the information technology, consumer discretionary, and communication services sectors and overweight financials, health care, and energy stocks.

Source: 2025 Annual Shareholder Report

Its recent performance has not been up to par (3 months and YTD) and goes beyond the hole left by expenses. But the key issue for longer-term holders has been the 2% drag since inception. We are not even talking about a drag versus NASDAQ or S&P 500. We are talking about a 2% drag vs. the buy-write index.

Of course, one could argue that the fund has beaten the same benchmark by the same amount over 5 years.

So "your mileage may vary" really applies here, and you may get different people arguing about this fund depending on when exactly they got involved.

Verdict

The fund solves three problems we have with most covered call funds. The first being that most covered call funds sell calls blindly at the front month. This results in whipsawing, and FTQI is bypassing that. The second problem is, of course, the index valuations today. They rest and have been resting in the 95th to 99th percentiles. FTQI, at least, is trying to actively manage out of that problem.

Finally, the fund does show some spine in bear markets, something that is sadly missing in most covered call funds.

That said, we can't recommend a buy here. The fund's longer-term performance has been a disappointment, and we can't fathom the rationale for going all-in on technology at this point in the cycle as part of active management. Even the top 10 stocks look eerily similar to those of Invesco QQQ Trust, Series 1 ETF (QQQ) today. The fund's option selling is better than most funds, but we prefer even longer-dated options. We rate this a hold.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, and sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are definitely not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: Healthcare Realty: Mispriced High-Yield Bargain
data.description:
It pays to have hard assets in a volatile market, especially when said assets generate a steady stream of income and are trading with a margin of safety. While big names in the healthcare space like Welltower (WELL) and Ventas (VTR) grab much of the attention, I see value in lesser-followed names that offer the best of both worlds: high yield/value and growth potential.

This brings me to Healthcare Realty Trust (HR), which I last covered in January, highlighting its robust fundamentals, attractive valuation, and strong growth outlook. HR has since delivered a 4% total return, far outpacing the 4% decline in the S&P 500 (SPY) over the same timeframe.

At the current price of $17.29, HR remains attractive with a forward P/FFO of 10.8 and a +5% dividend yield, as shown below.

In this article, I revisit HR, including recent business fundamentals, and discuss why it remains an attractive ‘Buy’ at present for income and value investors, so let’s dive in!

Why HR?

Healthcare Realty Trust is the only REIT to focus exclusively on owning, managing, and developing outpatient medical facilities across the U.S. It partners closely with leading health systems and physicians, providing mission-critical real estate that supports the ongoing shift from inpatient to outpatient care.

After an operational reset including a new dividend rate and portfolio repositioning, management now frames the business as “Healthcare Realty 2.0.” Under this model, HR emphasizes disciplined capital allocation and a streamlined portfolio concentrated in high-growth markets.

At present, HR has 562 properties covering 33 million square feet. Most of its annual base rent (90%) comes from the Top 50 MSAs in the U.S. As shown below, its top tenants are well-recognized health systems with mostly stellar credit ratings.

HR continued to demonstrate solid fundamentals during Q4 2025, with same-store NOI rising by 5.5% YoY. This marks an acceleration from SSNOI growth of 4.8% for the full year, due to better tenant retention and higher cash leasing spreads. This was supported by 5.8 million square feet of leasing activity during Q4, including 1.6 million square feet of new leases. Occupancy grew, as a result, by 103 basis points YoY to 92.1%, and tenant retention rose by 200 basis points YoY to 82%.

The strong results are supported by HR’s portfolio repositioning, in which it disposed of $1.2 billion worth of non-core assets at a blended 6.7% cap rate last year. This exceeded original guidance on both volume and pricing.

As a result, HR exited 14 non-core markets, sharpening its focus on high-growth metropolitan areas. Management believes what remains is a premier outpatient medical portfolio supported by same-store growth and rising demand fundamentals.

Looking ahead, management is guiding for SSNOI growth of 4% this year at the midpoint of the range and improving tenant retention to the low- to mid-80% range. Growth in the near term is also supported by internal investment in the existing portfolio. This involves transitioning decades-old buildings into near-new facilities with a target yield on cost of 10%.

HR is also supported by strong external demand drivers, not least of which includes a rapidly growing aging population. The age 65+ population is expected to grow by 20% over the next 10 years. This, combined with a shifting landscape toward outpatient care and lower new supply, all favors HR, as shown below.

Importantly, HR maintains a solid balance sheet with BBB/Baa2 credit ratings from S&P and Moody’s. This is supported by a safe net debt-to-adjusted EBITDA ratio of 5.4x and $1.4 billion in total liquidity. HR currently yields 5.6%, and the quarterly dividend rate, which was reduced by $0.07 last year, is well covered by a 60% payout ratio. This leaves HR with more retained capital, which it can use for internal development.

I continue to see value in HR at the current price of $17.29 with a forward P/FFO of 10.8, sitting far below its historical 16.1, as shown below.

At the current price, I believe the market is still valuing HR as the ‘old version.’ At the same time, it’s ignoring its portfolio repositioning and far better dividend safety with retained capital for internal growth funding. I believe HR could achieve an FFO/share growth rate of 5% annually over the medium term, which, when combined with the dividend and potential reversion to the mean valuation, could lead to market-beating total returns.

Risks to HR include execution risks around redevelopment, which could be hindered by construction delays and cost overruns. An uptick in new supply could put downward pressure on rent growth. Plus, higher interest rates could raise the cost of debt and reduce investment spreads.

Investor Takeaway

Healthcare Realty Trust stands out as a compelling income and value opportunity within the healthcare REIT space, especially compared to more widely followed peers. Following its strategic reset and portfolio repositioning, the company is now operating with improved fundamentals. This includes stronger tenant retention and a sharper focus on high-growth outpatient markets.

HR still trades at a notable discount to its historical valuation, offering a margin of safety alongside a well-covered 5%+ dividend yield. HR appears well-positioned to deliver attractive total returns with a solid balance sheet and favorable long-term tailwinds from aging demographics and the shift to outpatient care.

Considering all the above, I believe the current valuation does not fully reflect the improved business model. HR is an appealing Buy for investors seeking both income and upside potential.

data.title: Grid Dynamics Navigates GenAI Environment As Revenue Growth Rate Falters
data.description:
Investment Outlook

Grid Dynamics Holdings, Inc. (GDYN), has reported its Q4 2025 financial results, beating revenue and consensus EPS estimates.

I previously analyzed GDYN in April 2025 with a Hold outlook due to cautious client spending concerns.

The company's revenue growth rate is slowing, while gross margins are under pressure due to revenue mix costs and FX headwinds.

GenAI projects are an increasing focus for Grid, but progress there remains a challenge as clients continue to move beyond pilot trials at a slow rate.

I remain a neutral Hold on GDYN while the company, and the larger consulting industry as a whole, faces demand challenges in the face of their GenAI dreams.

Grid's Market And Approach

GDYN provides clients with various consulting services in the functional areas of technology strategy, engineering, and design.

The firm recognizes revenue from clients in various industry segments and regions, as shown in the pie charts below:

The company’s primary service offerings include:

  • Digital transformation.

  • Technical consulting.

  • Internet services operations.

  • Software design, development, and testing.

Grid seeks new clients via its direct sales and marketing efforts and through partner relationships.

A 2026 Business Research Insights market research report said that the global digital transformation consulting services market was an estimated $68 billion and was forecasted to reach $183.5 billion by 2035.

If achieved, this growth would represent a CAGR of 13.2% from 2026 to 2035, a fairly robust rate of growth for the consulting industry.

The expected trajectory for the digital transformation consulting industry through 2035 is shown on the chart below:

The primary drivers for industry growth are a continuing shift by organizations to various cloud-based environments, such as all-private, all-public, and hybrid clouds.

Customers are also showing increased interest in GenAI technologies to improve their operational efficiency, with early production use in the areas of customer service, procurement, and sales & marketing.

However, the IT Consulting market is subject to substantial fragmentation, with vendors of various sizes providing localized services and a variety of service bundles, depending on the region.

Significant industry participants include:

  • Accenture (ACN).

  • Globant (GLOB).

  • EPAM Systems (EPAM).

  • Deloitte Digital.

  • Thoughtworks (Acquired 2024).

  • Slalom.

  • McKinsey & Company.

  • BCG.

  • Cognizant Technology Solutions (CTSH).

  • Ideo.

  • Capgemini (CAPMF).

  • Computer Task Group (Acquired 2023).

  • Others.

While the technical consulting and digital transformation industry has fragmentation, scale continues to matter since larger firms can spread out delivery, marketing, and platform investment expenses across a larger customer and revenue base.

The most recent trend in the industry is a slow but steady shift from GenAI pilots to production uses, as clients begin to see repeatable improvements and cost efficiencies in customer service and management information systems.

The industry is also beginning to experiment with output- and outcome-based revenue generation, which can give firms with reusable platform IP an advantage in some engagements.

Grid’s strengths are its engineering focus, vertical specialization in retail, TMT, finance, and manufacturing, and proprietary platforms.

Weaknesses include its customer concentration among large customers, legacy business in short-duration structures, and its small scale, limiting its ability to amortize costs.

The firm’s opportunities ahead comprise a growing GenAI production backlog, recurring revenue platform offerings, and hyperscaler partnerships.

Threats to Grid’s potential are larger service firms’ increasing focus on GenAI projects, GenAI regulation that raises delivery complexities and costs, and maintaining leadership in a rapidly changing GenAI environment.

Recent Financial Trends

Topline revenue hit a record, but it was only up 1.9% sequentially and 5.9% year-over-year, while operating income rose only slightly due to foreign exchange headwinds.

Grid’s core operating leverage ratio continued to slide on increasing costs against tepid revenue growth.

Earnings per share fell to breakeven, the worst performance in the last six quarters, driven largely by lower gross margin and FX challenges.

Capital Allocation

As of the end of 2025, Grid had approximately $342 million in cash and equivalents and no debt.

Free cash flow for the calendar year 2025 was $25.3 million after capital expenditures of $15.3 million.

Stock-based compensation (SBC) was a hefty $30.3 million, so free cash flow net of SBC was negative ($5.0 million).

However, the company acquired a net of $19.7 million of its shares during 2025, so it offset about two-thirds of the SBC dilutive effect in the process.

As of the end of Q4 2025, the company had $48 million left on its Q3 stock buyback authorization of $50 million.

So, Grid’s capital allocation profile is strong from a balance sheet perspective but weak in terms of equity efficiency.

M&A remains the top capital allocation priority for the company, according to management, so the coming quarters may see continued acquisition activity, which may lead to lumpy costs and net results.

Why I'm Neutral On Grid Dynamics

Grid is operating in a difficult time for the consulting industry overall.

The industry has retained most of the headcount growth it ramped up during the pandemic and has had to spend considerable money retraining them for the GenAI era.

However, GenAI projects have primarily been pilot projects for cost-takeout purposes, not the transformational production engagements that provide more lucrative revenues for the consulting firms.

As a result, consulting firms have generally posted anemic topline revenue growth and disappointing earnings as they await client transitions to more valuable work projects.

They haven’t been helped by the rapidly changing nature of underlying GenAI technologies or their probabilistic characteristics that are at odds with the need for predictable, repeatable actions according to client company business rules, policies, and regulatory requirements.

The management teams of so many consulting firms continue to say how optimistic they are that conditions are changing, effectively asking investors to be patient and ‘hang in there,’ with better days just around the corner.

Yet the numbers repeatedly tell a different story: anemic topline revenue growth, poor earnings, static cash flow generation, and clients stuck in pilot projects that are still not producing acceptable enough results to shift to production.

The graphic below shows the frequency of various negative-oriented key phrases in management’s most recent conference call with analysts:

The chart shows that Grid is seeing YoY Retail and CPG/Manufacturing sector revenue declines and FX headwinds.

Looking ahead, management said they were ‘bullish’ on 2026’s revenues, yet if the midpoint of their new target is reached, revenue will grow only about 12% versus 17.5% for the prior year.

A 33% drop in revenue growth rate isn’t exactly my definition of ‘bullish.’
